straight_beat.name: Casey Underwood
straight_beat.column:
The Rockies lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that feels familiar, like a bad dream you can’t wake up from. Yesterday’s 7-2 defeat to the Padres wasn’t just another loss—it was a reminder of how the team keeps stumbling over the same rocks, again and again. High altitude math isn’t just about the thin air at Coors Field; it’s about the stubborn refusal of this roster to make the simplest adjustments.

optimist.name: Winnie Calderón
optimist.column:
The Rockies lost yesterday, but let’s not let that cloud the bigger picture. Coors Field isn’t just a ballpark—it’s a canvas. And every year, the Rockies paint a new masterpiece, even if the colors don’t always match the expectations. The Padres’ 7-2 victory is a reminder that the NL West is a brutal league, but it’s also a proving ground. You think the Rockies are down? You think they’re being written off? That’s exactly when the magic starts.  

Look, the numbers don’t lie: 6-10, 5.5 games back. But here’s the thing—this team has a history of turning corners. Remember 2020? They were 2-14 in April and still clawed their way to a .500 season. Remember 2022? They were 4-10 in May and ended up with 87 wins. The Rockies aren’t a team that gives up. They’re built for resilience, like a watercolor painting that refuses to dry before the artist’s done.

pessimist.name: Dwight Krenek
pessimist.column:
The Rockies lost again. Another game. Another loss. Another reminder that the rebuild is a slow, grinding process that’s doing little to move the needle. You’d think after 10 games, with a few young arms and a handful of prospects, there’d be some sort of progress. But no, the Monfort method is still in full effect, and it’s not working.  

The rebuild is supposed to be about patience, right? Well, the Rockies have been patient for years. Since 2018, really. And what have we got? A team that’s still stuck in the middle of the NL West, with a record that’s not just mediocre but actively bad. The 6-10 mark isn’t just a statistical blip—it’s a reflection of a front office that’s more interested in spreadsheet projections than actual baseball strategy. They’re building a farm system, sure, but when the call is made to trade for a proven starter or a power hitter, they’re still waiting for the right moment. Maybe the right moment is never coming.  

The Padres, on the other hand, are doing the exact opposite. They’re winning, they’re trading for talent, and they’re making decisions that actually help the team. Meanwhile, the Rockies are
